name: Pwyll
description: Pwyll Pendefig Dyfed, meaning "Pwyll, Head of Dyfed," is a prominent figure in Welsh mythology, primarily known from the First Branch of the Mabinogi. He is depicted not as a god in the traditional sense, but as a mortal king possessing extraordinary qualities and the ability to traverse between worlds. Pwyll's narrative explores themes of honor, responsibility, and the consequences of rash decisions. His encounter with Arawn, the ruler of Annwn (the Otherworld), sets the stage for a year-long exchange of identities and kingdoms, during which Pwyll must uphold Arawn's responsibilities and maintain his fidelity to his wife. Pwyll's wisdom and integrity during this trial solidify his reputation. He is also known for his later marriage to Rhiannon and the eventual birth of their son, Pryderi, marking a crucial lineage in Welsh mythology. Pwyll's story offers insights into early Welsh social structures, moral codes, and the perceived relationship between the human and supernatural realms. His actions demonstrate a balance between human fallibility and the potential for heroic virtue, making him a complex and compelling figure within the broader context of Celtic mythology.
subtitle: Head of Dyfed

keyMyths.title: The Exchange with Arawn
keyMyths.description: While hunting, Pwyll encounters Arawn, the ruler of Annwn, whose hounds are hunting a stag. After intervening, Pwyll agrees to exchange places with Arawn for a year and a day. During this time, Pwyll must rule Annwn with Arawn's appearance, and resist the advances of Arawn's wife. He succeeds, proving his honor and loyalty. This myth highlights the importance of keeping one's word and the potential for humans to embody kingly virtues.

keyMyths.title: Winning Rhiannon
keyMyths.description: Pwyll encounters Rhiannon riding a magnificent white horse. He sends his men to chase her, but she always remains just ahead. Finally, Pwyll himself calls out to her, and she stops, agreeing to marry him. This myth emphasizes Rhiannon's otherworldly nature and Pwyll's respect for her agency. It also demonstrates the importance of communication and respect in forming alliances and relationships.

keyMyths.title: The Birth and Loss of Pryderi
keyMyths.description: After years of marriage, Rhiannon gives birth to a son, Pryderi. On the night of his birth, the child is mysteriously stolen. Rhiannon is falsely accused of murdering him and forced to perform penance. However, Pryderi is later found and returned, restoring Rhiannon's honor. This myth explores themes of false accusations, the importance of lineage, and the restoration of justice through perseverance.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Scandinavian and Celtic Religions
sources.description: Explores the broader context of Celtic religious beliefs and practices, offering insights into the symbolism and cultural significance of figures like Pwyll.
